title: Short or Default Password Patterns in OT Authentication Logs

compliance.assurance_rationale: Auto-migrated from cat-22 markdown on Phase 1.3 run. Requires SME review to confirm clause accuracy, mode (satisfies vs detects-violation-of), and assurance level (contributing/partial/full).

spl:
(index=windows EventCode="4625" Status!="0x0") OR (index=fw sourcetype="fortigate_event" subtype="vpn")
| eval user=coalesce(Account_Name, user, vpnuser)
| where match(user,"(?i)hmi|operator|ot_")
| bin _time span=1h
| stats count as fails by _time, user, src_ip
| where fails>30
| sort - fails
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.src span=1h | sort - count

grandmaExplanation: Uses policy checks on authentication metadata (where available) and heuristics on failed logon bursts to catch weak password hygiene in OT accounts ‚Äî so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
implementation: (1) Never log cleartext passwords‚Äîuse failure reason codes only; (2) pair with AD password policy audit offline; (3) enforce lockout thresholds on jump hosts; (4) reset compromised accounts via PAM.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Windows TA (742), FortiGate TA.
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `WinEventLog:Security` 4625; `fortigate_traffic` or `sslvpn` auth failures.
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
(1) Never log cleartext passwords‚Äîuse failure reason codes only; (2) pair with AD password policy audit offline; (3) enforce lockout thresholds on jump hosts; (4) reset compromised accounts via PAM.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
(index=windows EventCode="4625" Status!="0x0") OR (index=fw sourcetype="fortigate_event" subtype="vpn")
| eval user=coalesce(Account_Name, user, vpnuser)
| where match(user,"(?i)hmi|operator|ot_")
| bin _time span=1h
| stats count as fails by _time, user, src_ip
| where fails>30
| sort - fails
```

Understanding this SPL

**Short or Default Password Patterns in OT Authentication Logs (IEC 62443-3-3 / SR 1.7)** ‚Äî Uses policy checks on authentication metadata (where available) and heuristics on failed logon bursts to catch weak password hygiene in OT accounts.

Documented **Data sources**: `WinEventLog:Security` 4625; `fortigate_traffic` or `sslvpn` auth failures. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Windows TA (742), FortiGate TA. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: windows, fw; **sourcetype**: fortigate_event. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=windows, index=fw, sourcetype="fortigate_event". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **user** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where match(user,"(?i)hmi|operator|ot_")` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚Ä¢ Discretizes time or numeric ranges with `bin`/`bucket`.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by _time, user, src_ip** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where fails>30` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.src span=1h | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Short or Default Password Patterns in OT Authentication Logs (IEC 62443-3-3 / SR 1.7)** ‚Äî Uses policy checks on authentication metadata (where available) and heuristics on failed logon bursts to catch weak password hygiene in OT accounts.

Documented **Data sources**: `WinEventLog:Security` 4625; `fortigate_traffic` or `sslvpn` auth failures. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Windows TA (742), FortiGate TA. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Authentication.Authentication` ‚Äî enable acceleration for that model.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Time chart (fail spikes), Table (user, src), Single value (distinct users).
visualization: Time chart (fail spikes), Table (user, src), Single value (distinct users).
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern ‚Äî correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
